title: Cloudflare WAF Managed Rule Block Rate by Rule ID (`cloudflare:json`)
dataSources: Cloudflare Logpush JSON delivered to Splunk HEC/S3 with `sourcetype=cloudflare:json` (HTTP requests, firewall, Workers, DNS, Access as configured)
app: Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501) + Logpush JSON (`cloudflare:json`)
spl:
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-24h
| spath RuleID
| spath Action
| where Action="block" OR match(Action,"(?i)deny")
| stats count by RuleID, ClientRequestHost
| sort -count
description: Managed rules evolve weekly; tracking blocks per rule ID shows tuning regressions and false-positive spikes after rule set upgrades.
value: Security engineering teams track which WAF managed rules are actively blocking traffic, catch false-positive regressions after weekly Cloudflare ruleset updates, and prioritize exception tuning before legitimate users or partner integrations are impacted.
implementation: Configure Logpush jobs per dataset; route all into a unified `cloudflare:json` sourcetype or use transforms to normalize. Use `spath` for PascalCase fields. Create macros per zone name.
visualization: Bar chart (rule ID), timechart (block rate), table (zone, path prefix).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501) v2.x installed on the Search Head for dashboards and field extractions. The app ships `props.conf` with `FIELDALIAS` mappings for the Cloudflare data model.
• Cloudflare Logpush configured for the **Firewall Events** dataset, delivering JSON to a Splunk HEC endpoint or S3 bucket. The Logpush job must include fields: `RuleID`, `Action`, `ClientIP`, `ClientRequestHost`, `ClientRequestURI`, `RayID`, `Source` (e.g. `firewallManaged`, `firewallCustom`). Use `sourcetype=cloudflare:json` and route to `index=waf`.
• Cloudflare plan: Pro or higher for WAF Managed Rules; Enterprise for full Logpush. Free plans only get sampled firewall analytics, not Logpush.
• Splunk role: users need `srchIndexesAllowed = waf`. HEC token must target `index=waf`.
• Create a `cloudflare_zones.csv` lookup: `zone_id,zone_name,owner_team,environment` for enrichment.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
In the Cloudflare dashboard: Analytics & Logs → Logpush → Create a job → Dataset: Firewall Events → Destination: Splunk (HEC endpoint URL, token, sourcetype `cloudflare:json`, index `waf`). Enable all available fields.

Verify data arrival:
```spl
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-15m
| spath Action | spath RuleID | spath Source
| stats count by Action, Source
```
You should see actions like `block`, `challenge`, `jschallenge`, `managedChallenge` with sources like `firewallManaged`, `firewallCustom`, `rateLimit`. If empty: check HEC token health in `_internal index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=HttpInputDataHandler`, verify the Logpush job status in the Cloudflare dashboard, and confirm the HEC endpoint is reachable from Cloudflare's push IPs.

Key fields: `RuleID` (managed rule identifier, e.g. `100001`), `Action` (block/challenge/log), `Source` (firewallManaged/firewallCustom/rateLimit), `ClientRequestHost`, `ClientIP`, `RayID`, `EdgeColoCode`.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — WAF managed rule block rate:
```spl
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-24h
| spath RuleID | spath Action | spath Source | spath ClientRequestHost
| where Action="block" AND Source="firewallManaged"
| stats count as blocks dc(ClientIP) as unique_ips by RuleID, ClientRequestHost
| sort - blocks
| head 30
```

Understanding this SPL: Filters to blocks from Cloudflare's managed ruleset (OWASP, Cloudflare Specials, leaked credentials, etc.), then ranks by volume. A rule that suddenly appears in the top-10 after a managed ruleset update likely indicates a false-positive regression — legitimate traffic matching a new or tightened signature. High `unique_ips` means broad impact; low `unique_ips` with high `blocks` means a single attacker or scanner.

Trending search — block rate over time:
```spl
index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-7d
| spath RuleID | spath Action | spath Source
| where Action="block" AND Source="firewallManaged"
| timechart span=1h count by RuleID limit=10 useother=f
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) In the Cloudflare dashboard: Security → Events → filter by Action = Block, Source = Managed Rules, last 24h. Compare total block count and top rule IDs against Splunk results.
(b) Pick a specific `RayID` from a blocked request in Cloudflare, search `index=waf RayID=<value>` in Splunk and confirm all fields match.
(c) Run `index=waf sourcetype="cloudflare:json" earliest=-24h | stats count by Source` to confirm you see all expected sources (firewallManaged, firewallCustom, rateLimit).

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard ("Cloudflare — WAF Managed Rules"):
• Row 1 — Single-value tiles: "Total blocks (24h)", "Unique rules triggered", "Top blocked zone".
• Row 2 — Timechart: block volume by rule ID, last 7 days, to spot ruleset update impact.
• Row 3 — Table: RuleID, zone, block count, unique IPs, sample URI paths.

Alert: Schedule every 4h over `-4h`. Trigger when any single RuleID exceeds 2x its 7-day average block rate — this catches false-positive regressions after managed ruleset updates. Route to the WAF tuning team.

Runbook:
1. **Spike after managed ruleset update** — Cloudflare updates managed rules weekly (Tuesdays typically). Compare the RuleID against the Cloudflare changelog. If false-positive: create a WAF exception for the specific URI/host combination. If true-positive: investigate the source IPs.
2. **Single rule blocking many unique IPs** — Likely a broad attack. Check if the rule targets a known CVE. Verify your origin is patched regardless.
3. **Rule blocking only internal/partner IPs** — Add the IP range to a Cloudflare IP Access Rule (allow) or create a WAF exception scoped to those IPs.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No firewall events in Splunk** — Logpush job may be paused or errored. Check Cloudflare dashboard → Logpush → job status. Common issue: HEC token expired or endpoint URL changed. Also verify the Logpush job dataset is "Firewall Events", not "HTTP Requests" (different dataset, different fields).
• **`RuleID` is empty** — The event source is not `firewallManaged`. Custom firewall rules and rate-limit rules use different field structures. Filter `Source="firewallManaged"` to isolate managed rules.
• **Block counts much higher in Splunk than Cloudflare dashboard** — Cloudflare dashboard samples at high volumes. Logpush delivers 100% of events. Splunk counts are authoritative.
• **Events arriving but `spath` returns null** — The JSON may be double-encoded or the Logpush output format changed. Check `index=waf earliest=-1m | head 1 | fields _raw` to inspect the raw payload structure.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on the protective rules at our web gateway so we can tell when a new rule starts blocking something it should not, and fix it before real visitors get turned away.
